Understanding Rust Ownership

Ownership is Rust's most unique feature and has deep implications for the rest of the language. It enables Rust to make memory safety guarantees without needing a garbage collector.

Each value in Rust has a variable that is called its owner. There can only be one owner at a time. When the owner goes out of scope, the value will be dropped.

The concept of ownership is central to understanding how Rust manages memory. Unlike languages with garbage collectors, Rust uses a system of ownership with a set of rules that the compiler checks at compile time.

Let's look at some examples of how ownership works in practice and how it interacts with functions, references, and slices.

Learn about borrowing Lifetimes explained References and slices Official Rust Book
